questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Weather Balloon Team
At 6:30 on a cold Saturday morning, a group of students gathered behind the county science center with a white balloon, a small instrument box, and a roll of orange tape. The project looked simple: fill the balloon with helium, attach the box, and let it rise. But the teacher leading the team, Mr. Sato, reminded them that the launch was only one part of the work. The real goal was to collect and interpret weather data.
The instrument box carried sensors that measured temperature, air pressure, humidity, and altitude. As the balloon rose, it sent information to a receiver on the ground. Students expected the temperature to drop steadily as the balloon climbed, but the data showed a brief layer where the air became warmer. Mr. Sato explained that this was a temperature inversion, a condition that can trap smoke, pollution, or fog near the ground.
The balloon eventually burst high in the atmosphere, as planned, and a small parachute carried the instrument box back to Earth. The team used a tracking signal to find it in a harvested cornfield twelve miles away. Back at the science center, the students graphed the data and compared it with the morning forecast.
The project did not turn students into professional meteorologists in one day. It did show them that weather reports are built from measurements, patterns, and careful interpretation. A forecast on a phone may look simple, but behind it is a long chain of observations.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A weather balloon project helped students understand how measurements are used to study weather.
choices.B: Students launched a balloon without collecting any useful information.
choices.C: Mr. Sato wanted students to stop using weather forecasts.
choices.D: The weather balloon failed because the instrument box was never recovered.

questions.correctAnswerText: A weather balloon project helped students understand how measurements are used to study weather.
questions.explanation: The passage shows students collecting weather data, graphing it, and connecting it to forecasts.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Weather Balloon Team.”
What was the main goal of the project?

choices.A: To collect and interpret weather data
choices.B: To decorate the science center
choices.C: To test how far students could walk in a cornfield
choices.D: To prove that phone forecasts are always wrong

questions.correctAnswerText: To collect and interpret weather data
questions.explanation: Mr. Sato explains that the launch is only part of the larger goal of collecting and interpreting data.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Weather Balloon Team.”
Which TWO kinds of information did the instrument box measure?

choices.A: Air pressure
choices.B: Humidity
choices.C: Student attendance
choices.D: Cornfield size

questions.correctAnswerText: Air pressure | Humidity
questions.explanation: The instrument box measured air pressure, humidity, temperature, and altitude.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Weather Balloon Team.”
What can be inferred from the temperature inversion described in the passage?

choices.A: Air temperature does not always change in a simple pattern as altitude increases.
choices.B: The balloon stopped sending information before it rose.
choices.C: Students measured only wind speed during the project.
choices.D: A temperature inversion means the forecast must be ignored.

questions.correctAnswerText: Air temperature does not always change in a simple pattern as altitude increases.
questions.explanation: The temperature inversion shows a layer where air became warmer instead of continuing to cool.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Weather Balloon Team.”
In the sentence “behind it is a long chain of observations,” the word observations most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=careful measurements and records
questions.explanation: In context, observations are measurements and records used to build forecasts.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Weather Balloon Team.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: The box measured temperature, air pressure, humidity, and altitude.
tiles.B: The data showed a brief layer where the air became warmer.
tiles.C: A tracking signal helped students find the box in a cornfield.
tiles.D: The students left the instrument box in the sky permanently.

targets.T1: Evidence that the project collected data
targets.T2: Evidence that the results included an unexpected pattern
targets.T3: Evidence that the equipment was recovered

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=The box measured temperature, air pressure, humidity, and altitude.; T2=The data showed a brief layer where the air became warmer.; T3=A tracking signal helped students find the box in a cornfield.
questions.explanation: Each tile matches the requested evidence.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Customer Callback Escalation Procedure
To: Customer Support Representatives and Team Leads
From: Service Operations Manager
Subject: Callback Escalation Procedure
Beginning February 4, customer support representatives must use the Callback Escalation Form when a customer has called about the same unresolved issue three or more times within seven days. The form is designed to prevent repeated calls from being treated as separate, unrelated problems.
Before completing the form, representatives should review the customer’s account notes, previous ticket numbers, promised actions, and any deadlines already given to the customer. If a previous ticket is still open, do not create a duplicate ticket. Add the new information to the existing ticket and mark it for team lead review.
The form requires the customer name, account number, original issue date, previous ticket numbers, summary of the unresolved issue, and the best callback window. Representatives should avoid blaming another department in the notes. Instead, describe the issue clearly and list what has already been attempted.
A team lead must review the escalation before the end of the shift. The goal is to give the customer one clear point of follow-up, reduce repeated explanations, and identify problems that need attention from another department.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain when and how representatives should escalate repeated unresolved customer issues
choices.B: To tell representatives to delete account notes after every call
choices.C: To require customers to open duplicate tickets for every problem
choices.D: To prevent team leads from reviewing customer complaints

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain when and how representatives should escalate repeated unresolved customer issues
questions.explanation: The document describes the form and process for repeated unresolved customer calls.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Customer Callback Escalation Procedure.”
When should representatives use the Callback Escalation Form?

choices.A: When a customer has called about the same unresolved issue three or more times within seven days
choices.B: Whenever a customer asks a question about store hours
choices.C: Only when a customer’s issue has already been solved
choices.D: Before reviewing any account notes

questions.correctAnswerText: When a customer has called about the same unresolved issue three or more times within seven days
questions.explanation: This is the condition that triggers the escalation form.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Customer Callback Escalation Procedure.”
Which TWO details are required on the form?

choices.A: The customer name
choices.B: The best callback window
choices.C: The representative’s favorite department
choices.D: The customer’s personal shopping preferences

questions.correctAnswerText: The customer name | The best callback window
questions.explanation: The form requires both the customer name and the best callback window.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Customer Callback Escalation Procedure.”
What should a representative do if a previous ticket is still open?

choices.A: Add new information to the existing ticket and mark it for team lead review.
choices.B: Create several duplicate tickets to increase visibility.
choices.C: Close the ticket without contacting anyone.
choices.D: Remove all earlier notes from the account.

questions.correctAnswerText: Add new information to the existing ticket and mark it for team lead review.
questions.explanation: The document says not to create a duplicate ticket when an earlier ticket is still open.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Customer Callback Escalation Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning February 4, customer support representatives must use the Callback Escalation Form.”
choices.B: “The form is designed to prevent repeated calls from being treated as separate, unrelated problems.”
choices.C: “Representatives should avoid blaming another department in the notes.”
choices.D: “A team lead must review the escalation before the end of the shift.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The form is designed to prevent repeated calls from being treated as separate, unrelated problems.”
questions.explanation: This sentence explains why the procedure exists.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Make Room for Workplace English Classes
Employers with many multilingual workers should consider offering voluntary English classes at the worksite before or after shifts. These classes would not replace job training or safety instruction, and they should not be used to pressure workers to give up their home languages. Instead, they would give employees another tool for communication, advancement, and confidence.
The benefits are practical. Workers who improve workplace English may feel more comfortable asking questions, reading notices, completing forms, or applying for promotions. Supervisors may also benefit when employees can discuss schedules, safety concerns, or customer requests more clearly. A small manufacturer in the region reported that after offering a twice-weekly class for six months, more employees applied for team leader training.
The program should be designed respectfully. Classes should be free, voluntary, and scheduled so that workers are not forced to choose between learning and family responsibilities. Employers could partner with adult education instructors who understand how to teach language for real work situations.
Some critics may argue that language classes are not an employer’s responsibility. However, employers already invest in communication when they provide manuals, training videos, and safety meetings. A voluntary English class is another way to reduce barriers and support workers who want to grow. When employees gain communication skills, both the worker and the workplace can benefit.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Employers should consider voluntary workplace English classes because they can support communication and advancement.
choices.B: Employers should require workers to stop using their home languages.
choices.C: English classes should replace safety training for all workers.
choices.D: Workers who speak more than one language should not apply for promotions.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employers should consider voluntary workplace English classes because they can support communication and advancement.
questions.explanation: The author argues that voluntary classes can help communication, confidence, and career growth.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Make Room for Workplace English Classes.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that classes can help advancement?

choices.A: More employees applied for team leader training after one manufacturer offered classes.
choices.B: Classes should not replace job training.
choices.C: Workers may have family responsibilities after shifts.
choices.D: Employers already provide manuals and safety meetings.

questions.correctAnswerText: More employees applied for team leader training after one manufacturer offered classes.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly connects English classes to advancement opportunities.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Make Room for Workplace English Classes.”

choices.A: Classes should be voluntary.
choices.B: Classes should be free.
choices.C: Workers should lose pay if they do not attend.
choices.D: Classes should replace all safety meetings.
choices.E: Workers should be banned from speaking other languages.

questions.correctAnswerText: Classes should be voluntary. | Classes should be free.
questions.explanation: The author recommends free and voluntary classes as part of respectful design.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Make Room for Workplace English Classes.”
How does the author respond to the idea that language classes are not an employer’s responsibility?

choices.A: By comparing language classes to other employer investments in communication
choices.B: By saying employers should stop providing manuals
choices.C: By claiming workers should never ask questions at work
choices.D: By arguing that home languages are a workplace problem

questions.correctAnswerText: By comparing language classes to other employer investments in communication
questions.explanation: The author argues that employers already support communication through manuals, videos, and meetings.

The training room has a whiteboard and twenty chairs.

questions.correct: D1=A worker survey found that many employees wanted to apply for higher positions but felt unsure about completing written applications in English.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A worker survey found that many employees wanted to apply for higher positions but felt unsure about completing written applications in English.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the claim that English practice could support advancement.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Make Room for Workplace English Classes.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Some workers want additional English practice to help them communicate and pursue opportunities.
choices.B: Every worker must speak only English to do a job well.
choices.C: Employers should stop hiring multilingual workers.
choices.D: Language instruction is useful only for children.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some workers want additional English practice to help them communicate and pursue opportunities.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that some workers would benefit from and want the classes.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Hemmed Jacket
Sofia had worked at the alteration shop for five months, mostly pressing seams and replacing simple buttons. She liked the order of the place: spools arranged by color, chalk marks brushed away at closing, pins returned to their tins. What she did not like was the row of jackets waiting for final hems. A sleeve could hide a mistake. A jacket hem sat in plain sight.
On Tuesday, Mrs. Patel placed a navy jacket on Sofia’s table. “Customer picks it up at four,” she said. “You measured this one yesterday. Now finish it.”
Sofia unfolded the jacket and found her own chalk line still visible. It looked too high, then too low, depending on how she held the fabric. She measured again. The number matched the ticket. Still, she hesitated.
Mrs. Patel watched without touching the jacket. “A good alteration is not a guess,” she said. “But it is also not fear. Measure, pin, check, stitch.”
Sofia pinned the hem and hung the jacket on the form. The fabric fell evenly. She checked the ticket again, then guided the cloth under the machine. The first line of stitches sounded louder than usual, though the shop radio played at the same volume as always.
At 4:10, the customer tried on the jacket and turned once before the mirror. “That’s exactly right,” he said.
Sofia looked at the hem. It was not invisible, but it belonged there. For the first time, the row of unfinished jackets looked less like a warning and more like work she could learn.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Sofia must overcome her fear of making a visible mistake while completing a jacket hem.
choices.B: Sofia tries to convince Mrs. Patel to close the alteration shop.
choices.C: Sofia argues with a customer about the shop radio.
choices.D: Sofia refuses to measure the jacket before stitching.

questions.correctAnswerText: Sofia must overcome her fear of making a visible mistake while completing a jacket hem.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Sofia’s hesitation and her effort to complete a difficult visible task.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Hemmed Jacket.”

choices.A: She believes a jacket hem makes mistakes easy to see.
choices.B: She does not know how to replace buttons.
choices.C: She wants to work only at the cash register.
choices.D: She thinks jackets should never be altered.

questions.correctAnswerText: She believes a jacket hem makes mistakes easy to see.
questions.explanation: Sofia thinks sleeve mistakes can be hidden, but jacket hems sit in plain sight.

choices.B: Sofia should stitch without checking measurements.
choices.C: Fear is the best way to finish difficult work.
choices.D: Customers do not notice whether hems are even.

questions.correctAnswerText: Careful work requires both accuracy and confidence.
questions.explanation: Mrs. Patel tells Sofia not to guess but also not to work from fear.

choices.C: She turns off the shop radio.
choices.D: She gives the customer a different jacket.
choices.E: She removes the chalk line without checking it.

questions.correctAnswerText: She measures the jacket again. | She hangs the jacket on the form to check how the fabric falls.
questions.explanation: These details show Sofia checking her work carefully.

choices.A: She begins to see difficult tasks as skills she can develop.
choices.B: She decides to stop working at the alteration shop.

choices.D: She wants Mrs. Patel to finish all future jackets.

questions.correctAnswerText: She begins to see difficult tasks as skills she can develop.
questions.explanation: Sofia starts to view the jackets as work she can learn rather than as warnings.

Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Sofia sees her chalk line and hesitates.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding a Pay Stub
A pay stub is more than a record of how much money was deposited into a worker’s account. It explains how gross pay becomes net pay. Gross pay is the amount earned before deductions. Net pay is the amount the worker receives after taxes, insurance, retirement contributions, and other deductions are taken out.
One useful section of a pay stub is the year-to-date column. It shows totals from the beginning of the year through the current pay period. A worker can use this column to check total earnings, taxes withheld, or contributions to a retirement plan. This information may be helpful when budgeting, applying for a loan, or preparing tax documents.
Another important detail is the difference between required and optional deductions. Payroll taxes are required by law. Health insurance premiums or retirement contributions may depend on choices the worker made during enrollment. If a deduction looks unfamiliar, the worker should ask payroll or human resources for an explanation rather than assuming it is correct.
Pay stubs can also reveal errors. Hours worked, overtime, paid leave, or benefit deductions may be listed incorrectly. Reviewing each stub soon after payday makes it easier to correct mistakes while records are fresh. A pay stub may look like a routine document, but it is an important tool for understanding earnings.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A pay stub helps workers understand earnings, deductions, totals, and possible errors.
choices.B: A pay stub shows only the amount deposited and no other information.
choices.C: Workers should ignore deductions because they are always correct.
choices.D: Net pay is the amount earned before any deductions are taken out.

questions.correctAnswerText: A pay stub helps workers understand earnings, deductions, totals, and possible errors.
questions.explanation: The passage explains gross pay, net pay, deductions, year-to-date totals, and error checking.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Pay Stub.”
What is gross pay?

choices.A: The amount earned before deductions
choices.B: The amount left after all deductions
choices.C: A fee paid to human resources
choices.D: A retirement account number

questions.correctAnswerText: The amount earned before deductions
questions.explanation: The passage defines gross pay as the amount earned before deductions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Pay Stub.”

choices.A: It shows totals from the beginning of the year through the current pay period.
choices.B: It lists only the worker’s favorite benefits.
choices.C: It replaces all tax documents automatically.
choices.D: It shows the worker’s future pay for next year.

questions.correctAnswerText: It shows totals from the beginning of the year through the current pay period.
questions.explanation: The year-to-date column shows cumulative totals for the year so far.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Pay Stub.”

choices.A: “A pay stub is more than a record of how much money was deposited into a worker’s account.”
choices.B: “Gross pay is the amount earned before deductions.”
choices.C: “Reviewing each stub soon after payday makes it easier to correct mistakes while records are fresh.”
choices.D: “Payroll taxes are required by law.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Reviewing each stub soon after payday makes it easier to correct mistakes while records are fresh.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly supports prompt review of pay stubs.

questions.prompt:

choices.B: It tells a story about one worker changing jobs.
choices.C: It ranks payroll departments from best to worst.
choices.D: It argues that workers should not read financial documents.

questions.correctAnswerText: It defines pay stub terms, explains useful sections, and gives advice about checking for errors.
questions.explanation: The passage is organized around definitions, pay stub sections, and practical advice.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Employee Parking Permit Reminder
(1) The office complex will issue new employee parking permits next month. (2) Employees should bring a driver’s license, vehicle registration, and current employee badge to the security desk. (3) Permits from the previous year will expire on March 31. (4) [D1], employees who park in the garage after that date must display the new permit. (5) The security desk [D2] open from 8:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m. during the first week of March. (6) Employees with temporary vehicles should ask security for a short-term pass.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “Therefore” shows the result of the expiration date, and “will be open” is grammatically correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Employee Parking Permit Reminder.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Employees with temporary vehicles should ask security for a short-term pass.
choices.B: Employees who have vehicles that are temporary should make a request to security for a pass that is short-term.
choices.C: Security should be asked by employees with temporary vehicles for a pass of short duration.
choices.D: Employees, if temporary vehicles are what they have, should ask for a temporary pass that is short-term.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employees with temporary vehicles should ask security for a short-term pass.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and keeps the original meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The permits in the front drawer needs new labels.
choices.B: The list of employee vehicles are on the desk.
choices.C: The guards who check the garage arrive before seven.
choices.D: The folder of registration copies have been filed.

questions.correctAnswerText: The guards who check the garage arrive before seven.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “guards” correctly takes the plural verb “arrive.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Jordan emailed Casey after the meeting, he attached the permit form.
choices.B: Jordan emailed Casey after the meeting, and Jordan attached the permit form.
choices.C: He attached it when Jordan emailed Casey after the meeting.
choices.D: After the meeting, he emailed him and attached it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Jordan emailed Casey after the meeting, and Jordan attached the permit form.
questions.explanation: Repeating “Jordan” makes clear who attached the form.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The security desk expected a long line on Monday morning. [D1], staff added an extra permit station in the lobby.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Therefore
questions.explanation: The extra permit station was added because a long line was expected.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The garage opens at six; however, permits are issued after eight.
choices.B: The garage opens at six however permits are issued after eight.
choices.C: The garage opens at six, however permits are issued after eight.
choices.D: The garage opens at six; however permits, are issued after eight.

questions.correctAnswerText: The garage opens at six; however, permits are issued after eight.
questions.explanation: A semicolon before “however” and a comma after it correctly connect the clauses.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.A: Because the permit line reached the hallway.
choices.B: After employees showed their registration.
choices.C: The security guard placed the permit on the windshield.
choices.D: While waiting near the lobby desk.

questions.correctAnswerText: The security guard placed the permit on the windshield.
questions.explanation: This is the only complete sentence.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The clerk checked the badge and prints the permit yesterday.
choices.B: The clerk checks the badge and printed the permit yesterday.
choices.C: The clerk checked the badge and printed the permit yesterday.
choices.D: The clerk had checked the badge and prints the permit yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The clerk checked the badge and printed the permit yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure?

choices.A: The form asks for vehicle make, license plate number, and whether the employee works full time.
choices.B: The form asks for vehicle make, license plate number, and employment status.
choices.C: The form asks for naming the vehicle, a plate number, and to show employment.
choices.D: The form asks for vehicle make, plates that are numbered, and working full time.

questions.explanation: The list uses parallel noun phrases.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Try a Four-Day Workweek Pilot
The accounting firm should test a four-day workweek for three months during its slower season. Employees would work four longer days and have Friday off, while client coverage would be handled through rotating on-call staff. The goal is not to reduce responsibility. The goal is to see whether a different schedule can maintain productivity while improving focus and morale.
A pilot program would provide useful evidence. Some employees say they lose time restarting tasks after frequent interruptions spread across five days. Longer blocks of focused work may help them complete reports and reviews more efficiently. A three-month test would allow managers to compare project completion, client response times, overtime, and employee feedback before making any permanent decision.
The firm can design the pilot carefully. Departments could stagger schedules if needed, set clear client response rules, and pause the pilot during deadline weeks. A limited test is less risky than making assumptions. If the data show problems, the firm can return to the current schedule. If the results are positive, the firm may gain a hiring advantage without increasing pay.
Passage B: Keep the Five-Day Schedule
The accounting firm should keep its current five-day schedule. A four-day workweek may sound attractive, but longer days can create fatigue, especially during work that requires accuracy and concentration. A tired employee reviewing financial records may be more likely to miss details. The firm should not trade one kind of stress for another.
Client service is another concern. Many clients expect questions to be answered Monday through Friday. Rotating on-call staff may help, but it could also create confusion about who is responsible for each account. If clients feel they are waiting longer for responses, the firm’s reputation may suffer.
The slower season is also when the firm trains staff, updates procedures, and prepares for busier months. Compressing the schedule could push these tasks aside. Instead of changing the workweek, managers should address interruptions directly by limiting unnecessary meetings, improving task assignment, and setting quiet work blocks. These changes would improve focus without disrupting client expectations or lengthening workdays.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports testing a four-day workweek, while Passage B argues the five-day schedule better protects accuracy and client service.
choices.B: Passage A argues that employees should stop serving clients, while Passage B argues that accounting work should end.
choices.C: Passage A supports cutting pay, while Passage B supports eliminating training.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on parking permits, while Passage B focuses on weather data.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports testing a four-day workweek, while Passage B argues the five-day schedule better protects accuracy and client service.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about whether a four-day pilot is worth testing.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the four-day workweek.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: A three-month test would allow managers to compare productivity, response times, overtime, and employee feedback.
choices.B: Longer days can create fatigue.
choices.C: Clients expect questions to be answered Monday through Friday.
choices.D: Training often happens during the slower season.

questions.correctAnswerText: A three-month test would allow managers to compare productivity, response times, overtime, and employee feedback.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports Passage A’s argument for a limited evidence-gathering pilot.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about the four-day workweek.

choices.A: Longer workdays could increase fatigue and reduce accuracy.
choices.B: Rotating on-call staff could confuse client responsibility.
choices.C: A four-day week would guarantee higher profits immediately.
choices.D: Employees never experience interruptions during the current schedule.
choices.E: Clients do not care when questions are answered.

questions.correctAnswerText: Longer workdays could increase fatigue and reduce accuracy. | Rotating on-call staff could confuse client responsibility.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims with concerns about accuracy and client service.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the four-day workweek.
Which statement from Passage A directly addresses the risk of making a permanent change too quickly?

choices.A: “The accounting firm should test a four-day workweek for three months during its slower season.”
choices.B: “The goal is not to reduce responsibility.”
choices.C: “Some employees say they lose time restarting tasks after frequent interruptions.”
choices.D: “Longer blocks of focused work may help them complete reports and reviews more efficiently.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The accounting firm should test a four-day workweek for three months during its slower season.”
questions.explanation: A temporary pilot directly addresses the risk of making a permanent change too quickly.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the four-day workweek.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors want the firm to improve work quality, but they disagree about whether a schedule pilot is the right method.
choices.B: Both authors think client service does not matter.
choices.C: Both authors argue that accounting work requires no concentration.
choices.D: Both authors believe training should be canceled.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors want the firm to improve work quality, but they disagree about whether a schedule pilot is the right method.
questions.explanation: Passage A supports a pilot, while Passage B supports addressing interruptions without changing the schedule.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the four-day workweek.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data from similar firms on productivity, error rates, client response times, and employee satisfaction during four-day pilots
choices.B: A list of office furniture purchased last year
choices.C: A history of accounting before computers
choices.D: A survey asking employees their favorite lunch foods

questions.correctAnswerText: Data from similar firms on productivity, error rates, client response times, and employee satisfaction during four-day pilots
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate both productivity benefits and risks.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Printed on bright yellow paper, Monique handed the permit instructions to each employee.

choices.A: Printed on bright yellow paper, Monique handed the permit instructions to each employee.
choices.B: Monique handed the permit instructions printed on bright yellow paper to each employee.
choices.C: Monique, printed on bright yellow paper, handed the permit instructions to each employee.
choices.D: Each employee printed Monique on bright yellow permit paper.

questions.correctAnswerText: Monique handed the permit instructions printed on bright yellow paper to each employee.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “permit instructions,” the item printed on yellow paper.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The firm sent a memo. The memo explained the pilot schedule.

choices.A: The firm sent a memo that explained the pilot schedule.
choices.B: The memo sent the firm and explained the pilot schedule.
choices.C: Explaining the firm, the pilot schedule sent a memo.
choices.D: The firm sent, and the pilot schedule explained the memo.

questions.correctAnswerText: The firm sent a memo that explained the pilot schedule.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The employee’s badge was scanned before the permit was issued.
choices.B: The employees badge was scanned before the permit was issued.
choices.C: The employees’ badge’s was scanned before the permit was issued.
choices.D: The employee badges’ was scanned before the permit was issued.

questions.correctAnswerText: The employee’s badge was scanned before the permit was issued.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the badge belongs to one employee.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Pay Employees for Required Training Time
Employers should pay employees for time spent completing required training. If a company requires workers to attend a course, watch a training module, renew a certification, or complete safety instruction, that time is part of the job. Employees should not have to give up unpaid evenings or weekends to meet requirements that benefit the workplace.
Paid training time promotes fairness. Hourly employees may have second jobs, childcare responsibilities, transportation limits, or school schedules. When required training is unpaid, the real cost is shifted onto workers. Some may delay completing training because they cannot afford the time. Others may rush through important material because they are trying to finish outside regular hours.
Paying for training can also help employers. Workers who complete training during scheduled time may be more focused and more likely to ask questions. Managers can track completion accurately and make sure everyone receives the same information. If the training is important enough to require, it is important enough to support properly.
Employers may worry about cost, but training is already an investment in safe and effective work. Paying employees for required training recognizes that investment honestly. Optional classes or personal career courses may be different, but mandatory job training should be treated as work time.
Passage B:
Allow Flexible Unpaid Training Options
Employers should not be required to pay for every hour employees spend on training. Many companies already provide paid training for essential tasks, but some required modules are short, self-paced, and easy to complete from home. Requiring all such time to be paid may create scheduling problems and reduce flexibility for both employers and workers.
Some employees prefer to complete brief training modules when it is convenient for them rather than stay late or attend a scheduled session. A worker might choose to finish a twenty-minute update after dinner instead of coming to work early. If every training task must be paid and scheduled, employers may require workers to complete modules only on site, which may be less convenient.
Cost is also a real concern for small businesses. Paying every employee for every short online training update, plus tracking the exact time, can create administrative burdens. These costs may reduce the number of optional development opportunities a company can offer.
A better policy would distinguish between major required training and brief updates. Employers should pay for long courses, certifications, and safety instruction that require significant time. For short refresher modules, flexible unpaid completion may be reasonable as long as deadlines are clear and employees are not overloaded.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether employers should pay employees for required training time? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.

questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
